steps.title: Identify what the whole passage is doing
steps.content: The passage describes two types of subjects Villasana portrays — famous figures (Frida Kahlo) and ordinary people (a woman with a baby) — then closes by stating she sees both approaches as depicting "power and interconnectedness of all people." The main idea must capture both subject types and their shared unifying purpose, not just one example or a detail about her career.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
Test each choice against the full passage:
  • Choice A — Villasana's works focus on recognizing both famous and everyday examples of human strength and connection.: names both subject types (famous + everyday), maps "power" to "strength" and "interconnectedness" to "connection," and reflects the unifying final sentence. ✓
  • Choice B — Villasana began her artistic career by painting portraits of famous people and then transitioned to depicting everyday people instead.: invents a career progression the passage never mentions — she does both, not one then the other. ✗
  • Choice C — Villasana's portrayal of a woman holding a baby focuses more on human connection than it does on human resilience.: narrows to a single work and makes a comparison the passage does not draw. ✗
  • Choice D — Villasana's depiction of Frida Kahlo receives more attention from scholars than her depiction of a woman holding a baby does.: introduces scholarly attention, which the passage never mentions. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
